one_liner: An 18-year-old college freshman is brought to the ED by his roommate after 12 hours of high fever, worsening headache, neck stiffness, and a new spreading rash on his trunk and legs.

stage2.hpi_extension: He felt well yesterday morning. Over the last 12 hours he developed a severe holocephalic headache, fever to 39.6°C, neck stiffness, photophobia, and one episode of vomiting. The roommate noticed several small red-purple spots on his arms and trunk this morning that did not blanch when pressed. He lives in a freshman dormitory.
stage2.pmh: Healthy. Vaccinations 'up to date through high school' but he is not sure about a meningococcal booster.
stage2.social_history: College freshman in a dormitory. Non-smoker.
stage2.physical_exam: General: Acutely ill-appearing, lethargic but arousable. HEENT: Photophobic. Pupils equal and reactive. Neck: Marked nuchal rigidity. Positive Kernig sign (resistance/pain on knee extension with hip flexed). Positive Brudzinski sign (involuntary hip and knee flexion when the neck is passively flexed). Cardiovascular: Tachycardic, regular. Pulmonary: Clear. Abdomen: Soft. Skin: Numerous non-blanching petechiae and several purpuric lesions on the trunk, arms, and lower extremities. Neuro: Lethargic, oriented x2, no focal deficits.

lab_results.result: Opening pressure markedly elevated (350 mm H₂O). Appearance: cloudy/turbid. WBC 4,200/µL with 92% neutrophils. Protein 280 mg/dL (markedly elevated). Glucose 18 mg/dL (markedly low; CSF/serum ratio < 0.4).
lab_results.clinical_significance: The CSF profile of bacterial meningitis: high opening pressure, neutrophilic pleocytosis, very high protein, and very low glucose. Viral meningitis would show a lymphocytic pleocytosis with normal glucose and only mildly elevated protein.

lab_results.result: CSF Gram stain: numerous gram-negative diplococci (kidney-bean shaped). Culture growing on Thayer-Martin medium. Two sets of blood cultures positive for Neisseria meningitidis.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Gram-negative diplococci on CSF Gram stain are essentially pathognomonic for Neisseria meningitidis. (Compare: gram-positive lancet-shaped diplococci → S. pneumoniae; gram-negative coccobacilli → H. influenzae; gram-positive rods in newborns/elderly → Listeria.)

lab_results.result: WBC 22 × 10³/µL with marked left shift. Platelets 88 × 10³/µL (low). PT/PTT prolonged, fibrinogen low, D-dimer elevated → early DIC. Mild metabolic acidosis with elevated lactate.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Marked leukocytosis with left shift confirms a severe bacterial infection. Thrombocytopenia with coagulation abnormalities reflects DIC, a feature of meningococcemia, which can progress to Waterhouse-Friderichsen syndrome (bilateral adrenal hemorrhage and adrenal insufficiency).

stage5.final_clue: Empiric IV ceftriaxone and vancomycin had been started immediately after blood cultures were drawn (do NOT wait for the LP to start antibiotics in suspected bacterial meningitis). Once N. meningitidis is confirmed, the team narrows therapy to ceftriaxone and arranges rifampin or ciprofloxacin chemoprophylaxis for his roommate and other close contacts.

debrief.classic_presentation: Bacterial meningitis classically presents with the triad of fever, headache, and neck stiffness (nuchal rigidity), often with photophobia, vomiting, and altered mental status. Kernig sign (pain/resistance with knee extension when the hip is flexed) and Brudzinski sign (involuntary hip/knee flexion on passive neck flexion) are classic but neither sensitive nor specific. Neisseria meningitidis is the classic cause in adolescents and young adults living in close quarters (college dorms, military barracks) and uniquely produces petechial/purpuric rash from meningococcemia. Most common bacterial causes by age: Newborn (<1 month): Group B Strep (S. agalactiae), E. coli, Listeria. Children (1 month–18 years): S. pneumoniae, N. meningitidis. Adults (18–60): S. pneumoniae, N. meningitidis. Elderly (>60) or immunocompromised: S. pneumoniae, Listeria, gram-negative rods.
debrief.pathophysiology: N. meningitidis is a gram-negative diplococcus with a polysaccharide capsule (the basis of vaccination). It colonizes the nasopharynx and invades through the mucosa into the bloodstream, where the lipooligosaccharide (LOS) endotoxin and capsule produce a profound systemic inflammatory response, septic shock, and DIC. The organism crosses the blood–brain barrier to infect the meninges, where neutrophils flood the subarachnoid space producing the characteristic CSF pattern. Severe meningococcemia can produce Waterhouse-Friderichsen syndrome — bilateral adrenal hemorrhage with acute adrenal insufficiency — which is rapidly fatal without treatment.
debrief.treatment_overview: Empiric antibiotics must be given as soon as bacterial meningitis is suspected — do NOT delay for the LP if it is going to take time. Empiric coverage for adults: vancomycin + ceftriaxone (add ampicillin for Listeria coverage in patients <1 month, >50 years, or immunocompromised). Once N. meningitidis is confirmed, narrow to ceftriaxone or penicillin G. Dexamethasone is given before/with the first dose of antibiotics for suspected pneumococcal meningitis (reduces hearing loss and mortality). Chemoprophylaxis for close contacts of meningococcal cases: rifampin, ciprofloxacin, or ceftriaxone (single dose). Vaccination: MenACWY (conjugate) for all adolescents and high-risk adults; MenB for outbreaks and at-risk groups.
high_yield_pearls: Bacterial meningitis CSF: ↑↑ opening pressure, neutrophil predominance, ↑↑ protein, ↓↓ glucose. Viral meningitis: lymphocytes, normal glucose, mildly ↑ protein.
Causes by age — Newborn: GBS, E. coli, Listeria. Kids/teens: S. pneumoniae, N. meningitidis. Elderly/immunocompromised: add Listeria.
N. meningitidis: gram-negative diplococcus, polysaccharide capsule, LOS endotoxin. Petechial/purpuric rash + meningitis = meningococcemia.
Waterhouse-Friderichsen syndrome: bilateral adrenal hemorrhage and acute adrenal insufficiency from severe meningococcemia.

Close-contact prophylaxis for N. meningitidis: rifampin, ciprofloxacin, or ceftriaxone (single dose). Empiric meningitis treatment in adults: vancomycin + ceftriaxone (+ ampicillin if Listeria possible).
sources: First Aid for the USMLE Step 1 (2017) — Microbiology chapter, 'Neisseria meningitidis' and 'Gram-negative diplococci' sections.
